coaching.coaching: Pin down done conditions for each acceptance criterion with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: done conditions for each acceptance criterion

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the floor-attribution analysis step, specify the concrete mechanism for disabling the hard-override floor (e.g. name the function parameter, a helper script invocation, or a temporary monkey-patch pattern) so the executor has an unambiguous starting point rather than having to reverse-engineer decide.py.
evidence: 'recompute impact_plan from each finding's persisted verification.severity_attributes with the hard-override floor disabled' — no script, flag, or helper function is named
Contrast with step 3 which gives a concrete invocation: 'python docs/experiments/calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py --impact-model-version plan-v2'
The hard-override floor is in src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py::impact_plan but no mechanism to toggle it is described
findings.finding: The floor-attribution analysis step instructs the executor to 'recompute impact_plan from each finding's persisted verification.severity_attributes with the hard-override floor disabled,' but provides no script, CLI flag, or code pointer for how to disable the floor and rerun the computation. An executing agent cannot proceed without independently discovering the code path in decide.py and writing a bespoke re-execution harness, making this step a 'figure out how' gap.
findings.location: What section, step 6 (floor-attribution analysis) and AC4
scenarios: An automated agent or new contributor cannot produce AC4's comment without first reverse-engineering decide.py and writing unreferenced analysis code, potentially computing the metric incorrectly.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'Disable the floor by calling impact_plan(..., override_floor=False) [or equivalent flag — confirm the exact parameter from decide.py] in a standalone script docs/experiments/floor_attribution_analysis.py that iterates over the plan-v2 segment's block findings and writes the results to a JSON artefact for reporting.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that explicitly covers the NO-GO path: e.g. 'If the GO/NO-GO floor is not met, a comment recording the low-power decision (segment size, reason for stop) is posted to this ticket and the ticket is closed with that comment as the sole deliverable.'
evidence: 'otherwise stop and record a low-power decision on this ticket' — this is described work with no AC counterpart

All four ACs presuppose the GO path: AC1 refers to 'extended script' output, AC2 to 'every non-DET blocking-tier criterion', AC3 to a Gerrit merge, AC4 to the floor-attribution analysis — none fire meaningfully on the NO-GO path
findings.finding: The 'What' section describes a NO-GO branch ('otherwise stop and record a low-power decision on this ticket') that has no covering Acceptance Criterion. No AC verifies that, when the corpus pre-check fails, the low-power decision is durably recorded and the ticket is considered done in that state.
findings.location: What section, bullet 1 (GO/NO-GO floor description); Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs implicitly assume GO path)
scenarios: If the plan-v2 segment falls below 500 sidecars at run time, or MIN_N<25 criteria are too few, the implementer stops — but has no verifiable acceptance criterion to mark the ticket done, creating ambiguity about closure.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC0 (or prepend to AC1): 'IF the corpus pre-check fires NO-GO (plan-v2 segment <500 or fewer than 5 criteria reach MIN_N=25), a comment on this ticket records the segment size, the specific failing condition, and a low-power decision rationale; remaining ACs are waived and the ticket is closed on that comment alone.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out statement for criteria_routing.json threshold changes — e.g. 'If post-merge block-rate monitoring (within N days) shows regression, the change is reverted via Gerrit revert of the same CL; calibration 4 re-adjudicates.'
evidence: 'criteria_routing.json threshold edits iff the adjudication warrants any' — the plan acknowledges the change but provides no explicit rollback mechanism
T4 requires: 'The remedy is an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract — acknowledgement alone is insufficient'
No mention of 'revert', 'back-out', 'expand-contract', or 'feature flag' anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan conditionally modifies src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json threshold values (a behavioral change to the blocking review system) but does not state an explicit rollback or back-out path if the changed thresholds prove incorrect after merging.
findings.location: What section, bullet 5 ('The edit target for threshold values is src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json'); AC3
scenarios: A threshold is lowered (LOWER/DEMOTE decision) and post-merge monitoring shows an unacceptable drop in catch rate; without a stated rollback path the team has no pre-agreed procedure to revert quickly.
A threshold is raised and causes a surge in blocking findings; the absence of a rollback plan creates operational confusion.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section bullet 5 or a new 'Rollback' subsection, add: 'If post-merge 7-day block-rate or validity signals regress beyond [threshold], the Gerrit change is reverted atomically (CL revert restores all three files: script, calibration doc, criteria_routing.json); a follow-up calibration-4 ticket is opened with the regression evidence.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit handling in the floor-attribution analysis for findings where `verification.severity_attributes` is absent or structurally incomplete: define whether such findings are excluded from the denominator (and reported as a separate 'unanalyzable' count) or treated as non-floor-driven, and surface the count of skipped findings alongside the floor-driven share in the AC4 comment.
evidence: Plan text: 'recompute impact_plan from each finding's persisted verification.severity_attributes with the hard-override floor disabled'
No handling stated for missing/null severity_attributes on individual findings
835 untagged sidecars exist in the corpus; some plan-v2 sidecars may predate the attribute field
findings.finding: The floor-attribution analysis (AC4 / 'What' §6) instructs recomputing impact_plan from each finding's `persisted verification.severity_attributes`, but states no handling for findings where that field is absent or structurally incomplete (e.g., pre-tagging sidecars that lack the attribute entirely). A silent zero-fill or a hard crash would both corrupt the floor-driven block count/share reported to story 7d91-55fc-e07c-4101 without any surfaced warning.
findings.location: What §6 (floor-attribution analysis) and AC4
scenarios: A finding sidecar tagged plan-v2 was written before verification.severity_attributes was added to the schema — the recompute silently treats it as non-floor-driven, understating the floor-driven share
A malformed attribute dict causes an uncaught KeyError mid-loop, truncating the count with no warning in the comment posted to 7d91-55fc-e07c-4101
findings.suggested_fix: Before the recompute loop, filter findings into analyzable (severity_attributes present and schema-valid) vs unanalyzable; report floor_driven/analyzable_n (unanalyzable: k) so the dependent story knows the coverage of the analysis.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a rollback plan for criteria_routing.json threshold edits: either (a) document the Gerrit revert command + who is responsible, or (b) note that thresholds ship behind a config flag, or (c) explicitly state the acceptable window between this ticket's merge and large-sleepful-needlefish shipping, so that a threshold raise does not go live in production without the floor-split mitigation.
evidence: Plan text: 'criteria_routing.json threshold edits iff the adjudication warrants any'
Plan text: 'MERGED (LLM-Review +1, Verified +1)' — merge is the only lifecycle event described
No revert procedure, no feature-flag, no staged rollout mentioned
The dependent story large-sleepful-needlefish depends on AC4 evidence, meaning threshold changes could go live before the floor-split mitigation is in place
findings.finding: The plan instructs editing `criteria_routing.json` (a shared production config that immediately affects all plan reviews upon Gerrit merge) but states no rollback plan, no staged-rollout, and no stated tolerance window if the adjudicated thresholds prove incorrect after merge — the consuming system (plan review) has no described fallback.
findings.location: What §5 ('The edit target for threshold values is src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json') and AC3
scenarios: Adjudicated thresholds are merged and immediately raise block rates on live plan reviews before the dependent floor-split story ships, with no quick-revert procedure documented
A mis-adjudicated threshold (e.g., a spurious RAISE) goes unnoticed until calibration 4 because no rollback path was defined

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the source citation: replace the reference to 0730-6ceb-88c3-430c comments with the actual artifact(s) that record the block-rate baseline (~25% pre-cal-2) and post-cal-2 figures (52-67%), or remove the unverifiable ~25% pre-cal-2 baseline claim.
evidence: Ticket 0730-6ceb-88c3-430c comments: both comments are exclusively about score_novelty probe results and carryover discrimination — no block-rate data
Epic d195 description says '~50-67% per-round BLOCK rate since calibration 2' but does not mention a '~25% pre-cal-2' baseline
The pre-cal-2 baseline figure (~25%) is not found in either referenced artifact
findings.finding: The plan cites 'comments of ticket 0730-6ceb-88c3-430c (analysis task)' as the grounded source for the block-rate rise (~25% pre-cal-2 to 52-67% after), but reading ticket 0730-6ceb-88c3-430c shows its comments are entirely about a novelty-scorer probe — no block-rate figures appear there. The actual block-rate data (~50-67% since cal-2) appears in epic d195's description; the '~25% pre-cal-2' figure appears nowhere in either ticket.
findings.location: Plan preamble: 'recorded in the comments of ticket 0730-6ceb-88c3-430c (analysis task) and epic d195's evidence trail - that analysis is the grounded source for the motivation numbers'
scenarios: An implementer relying on 0730's comments to reproduce the motivation numbers cannot find the cited evidence; if audited, the calibration record's stated motivation is unverifiable from the named source
The motivating block-rate claim may be partially fabricated — if the pre-cal-2 baseline is wrong, the delta justifying this calibration is unverifiable

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the BLOCK-ELIGIBLE rounding rule with the script: either update `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py` line 235 to `round(round(r['p90'] / 0.05) * 0.05, 2)` (nearest 0.05) so the script's output matches the stated rule, or change the plan's rule to 'nearest 0.01' to match what the script actually produces.
evidence: Script line 235: `thr = max(0.5, min(0.95, round(r['p90'], 2)))` — rounds to 2 decimal places (nearest 0.01)
Plan text: 'adopt max(0.5, p90 rounded to nearest 0.05) when it differs from the current threshold by >=0.05 in either direction'
The script prints its threshold with `{thr:>10.2f}` format, confirming 2-decimal output
findings.finding: The plan's BLOCK-ELIGIBLE decision rule says 'adopt max(0.5, p90 rounded to nearest 0.05)', but the actual script (`calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py` line 235) computes `max(0.5, min(0.95, round(r['p90'], 2)))` — rounding to the nearest 0.01, not the nearest 0.05. The adjudicator will receive script output rounded to 2 decimal places but the plan's stated adoption rule refers to 0.05 granularity, creating an ambiguity about how to translate the script's proposed threshold into the `criteria_routing.json` edit.
findings.location: What section, bullet 4 (adjudication decision rule, BLOCK-ELIGIBLE branch): 'adopt max(0.5, p90 rounded to nearest 0.05)'
scenarios: Script proposes p90=0.63 → the plan's rule says round to nearest 0.05 → 0.65; but the executor reads 0.63 from the script and writes 0.63 to criteria_routing.json — a different value than the rule intends
Multiple criteria in the BLOCK-ELIGIBLE class could land at fine-grained thresholds (0.61, 0.63, 0.67) that the rounding rule would have coalesced to fewer, more legible values
findings.suggested_fix: Update script line 235 to: `thr = max(0.5, min(0.95, round(round(r['p90'] / 0.05) * 0.05, 2)))`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py` (and `tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py` for any posture change) to the Gerrit change's required co-edit list: any `criteria_routing.json` threshold or posture edit must update the inline `EXPECTED_ROUTING` table and `_PLAN_REVIEW_APPROVED_BLOCKING` set in the same commit, or CI will fail.
evidence: tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py line 25: EXPECTED_ROUTING = { … } (full table pinned inline, including every block_threshold)
tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py line 116: assert actual == EXPECTED_ROUTING

tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py line 103: _PLAN_REVIEW_APPROVED_BLOCKING = frozenset({'COH', 'E2', 'E4', 'F1', 'G1G2', 'G5', 'G6', 'T1', 'T4', 'T5e', 'T8'}) — a DEMOTE decision would also break this
Plan: 'The edit target for threshold values is src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json' — no mention of test updates
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py` pins the COMPLETE `(block_threshold, default_posture)` routing table via an inline `EXPECTED_ROUTING` dict (line 25) and also asserts the exact blocking set. Any `criteria_routing.json` threshold edit — which the plan explicitly contemplates — will break this test. The plan does not enumerate this test file as a consumer requiring update in the same Gerrit change.
findings.location: What section, bullet 5 ('criteria_routing.json threshold edits iff the adjudication warrants'); AC3 ('MERGED … criteria_routing.json threshold edits')
scenarios: Adjudication lowers a threshold (e.g. T1 from 0.70 to 0.65) and the Gerrit change passes criteria_routing.json validation but fails test_threshold_recalibration.py::test_full_routing_table_matches_pinned_expectation, blocking Verified +1

A DEMOTE decision changes default_posture of a criterion from blocking to advisory, breaking both test_threshold_recalibration.py and test_impact_model_versioning.py::test_plan_review_blocking_posture_set_is_the_approved_set
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section bullet 5, add: '…and updating the inline EXPECTED_ROUTING table in tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py (and _PLAN_REVIEW_APPROVED_BLOCKING in test_impact_model_versioning.py for any posture change) in the same Gerrit change.' Reflect in AC3 as well.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale (1–3 sentences) in the What section or preamble explaining why offline replay from versioned REVIEW_RESULT sidecars is the right mechanism for this calibration (e.g., zero LLM cost, reproducible, controls for model-output variance by using persisted verifier outputs, and the sidecar corpus is large enough to reach MIN_N on most criteria).
evidence: The preamble explains the problem and urgency ('Calibration 2 … lowered six criteria … adjudicated against the OLD mean impact model; the plan-v2 … has never been replayed') but gives no affirmative statement of why offline replay from persisted sidecars is the chosen mechanism.

The 'What' section jumps directly to corpus pre-check and script extension steps without articulating the positive case for the approach (zero-cost, reproducible, controlled, avoids confounders from live model-output variance).
Per G6.positive_rationale: 'The plan states a POSITIVE rationale for the chosen approach (its absence is a finding).'
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for why the offline sidecar-replay mechanism (rather than alternative approaches such as prospective live re-review or a pure threshold-sweep on synthetic data) is the right tool for this calibration. The 'What' section describes the steps but never affirmatively explains why this approach is fit-for-purpose.
findings.location: ## What (first and third bullets) / preamble paragraph
scenarios: An implementer reading the plan cannot distinguish between 'this approach was chosen deliberately over alternatives' and 'this is the only approach anyone considered' — a future calibration author may not know why this mechanism was selected.
Without a stated rationale, a reviewer cannot judge whether the approach is sound relative to alternatives — this is the very purpose of G6.positive_rationale.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' preamble or the first bullet: 'Offline replay from persisted REVIEW_RESULT sidecars is the correct mechanism here: it is zero-cost (no live LLM calls), fully reproducible, and controls for finder/verifier model-output variance by using the verifier outputs already emitted under plan-v2 — the only confound removed is the threshold, which is exactly what we are calibrating.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the interface by which load()'s skipped counters reach main() — e.g., change load()'s return type to a NamedTuple/dataclass carrying `(by_ticket, skipped_different_version: int, skipped_untagged: int)` — so the counter placement is unambiguous.
evidence: Plan: 'Extend docs/experiments/calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py so load() counts skipped sidecars by reason (different-version vs untagged) and main() prints the skipped remainder alongside the segment size'
load() signature in calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py:34: `def load(impact_model_version: str | None = None) -> dict[str, list[dict]]` — returns only the bucketed sidecar dict, no counter output
The continue statement at line 51 silently discards skipped sidecars with no accumulation side-effect; there is no shared counter state or altered return type specified in the plan
findings.finding: The skipped-counter is placed ambiguously: the plan instructs extending load() to count skipped sidecars by reason, then separately instructs main() to print those counts — but load() currently returns only dict[str, list[dict]], with no specified mechanism to surface the counters to main().
findings.location: What bullet 2 / AC1 — 'Extend docs/experiments/calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py so load() counts skipped sidecars by reason … and main() prints the skipped remainder'
scenarios: An executor adds two module-level counters (a pink-elephant antipattern for a pure function), making the script non-reentrant and breaking tests that call load() directly.
An executor changes the return type to a tuple or NamedTuple, breaking any caller that destructures the return value as a plain dict.
An executor prints the counts inside load() (violating single-responsibility), making AC1's 'main() prints' claim false.
findings.suggested_fix: Change `load()` to return a lightweight dataclass or NamedTuple: `LoadResult(by_ticket: dict[str, list[dict]], skipped_different_version: int, skipped_untagged: int)`. Increment the two counters inside the existing `continue` branch (one per skip reason). main() unpacks the result and prints both counts.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a tiebreaker protocol for the FP-PRONE fork — e.g., 'DEMOTE when the criterion is currently non-blocking (posture=advisory at 0.95); RAISE threshold when the criterion is currently blocking and raising to >=0.90 is less disruptive than removing blocking posture entirely' — so the executor has a deterministic rule.
evidence: Plan verbatim: 'script class FP-PRONE (mean validity <0.45 or P(indeterminate)>0.20) -> DEMOTE to advisory OR raise threshold to >=0.90, choose and record which'
No other section of the plan supplies the decision rule for choosing between the two branches
The calibration doc (docs/experiments/plan-review-threshold-calibration.md) will record this decision durably, so the choice matters for future calibrations
findings.finding: The FP-PRONE adjudication branch presents a binary fork ('DEMOTE to advisory OR raise threshold to >=0.90, choose and record which') with no protocol specifying when to choose demotion versus threshold-raise, leaving the decision undefined for an executor who must durably record it in the calibration doc.
findings.location: What bullet 4 — adjudication decision rule, clause (b): 'script class FP-PRONE (mean validity <0.45 or P(indeterminate)>0.20) -> DEMOTE to advisory OR raise threshold to >=0.90, choose and record which'
scenarios: Two executors independently adjudicate the same FP-PRONE criterion differently (one demotes, one raises threshold), producing incomparable calibration records.
The executor defaults to one branch for all FP-PRONE criteria without considering the semantic difference, undermining the precision-first intent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'ambiguous' classification boundary explicitly — e.g., 'ambiguous = the finding text neither clearly asserts absence of any verification method NOR clearly identifies an existing check missing a specific parameter; classify ambiguous when both missing_oracle and underspecified_oracle readings are equally plausible' — so classifiers apply it consistently.
evidence: Plan defines: missing_oracle = 'text asserts no verification method exists'; underspecified_oracle = 'text demands a more specific command, file, or expected value for a check that exists'; ambiguous = no definition given beyond the category name
Search for 'missing_oracle' and 'underspecified_oracle' in the entire repo returns zero hits — these categories exist only in the plan text, not in any schema or codified rubric
The plan's AC4 reports 'the missing_oracle/underspecified_oracle/ambiguous proportions' — the proportions are the deliverable, so classification consistency matters
findings.finding: The three-way classification enum for the floor-attribution analysis (missing_oracle / underspecified_oracle / ambiguous) is introduced only in the plan text and has no boundary condition specified for the 'ambiguous' bucket — an executor classifying up to 100 findings needs a decision rule distinguishing 'ambiguous' from the other two categories.
findings.location: What bullet 6 — floor-attribution analysis: 'Classify the ac_unverifiable!=none floor-driven findings\' finding text into missing_oracle … / underspecified_oracle … / ambiguous'
scenarios: A finding says 'the AC cannot be verified without running a live environment' — is this missing_oracle (no method exists) or underspecified_oracle (a method exists but the command is unspecified)? Without a boundary rule, two classifiers disagree.

The 'ambiguous' bucket absorbs all difficult cases inconsistently, inflating its proportion and making the missing_oracle/underspecified_oracle split unreliable as design input for story 7d91-55fc-e07c-4101.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback rule for findings whose persisted severity_attributes lack all seven plan-severity axes — e.g., 'exclude such findings from the floor-attribution denominator and report the exclusion count' — so the floor-driven share is not silently understated.
evidence: decide.py:166: `contribs = [_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) for a in _PLAN_SEVERITY_AXES]` — a missing axis silently maps to 0.0
decide.py:167: `impact_sev = max(contribs) if contribs else 0.0` — if ALL axes are absent, impact_sev = 0.0
The sidecar stores 'verification' as an opaque dict; for plan-v2 sidecars from before PlanSeverityAttrs shipped, the seven plan-severity axes will all be absent from severity_attributes
Plan text: no mention of how to handle findings with empty/absent severity_attributes in the floor-attribution recompute
findings.finding: The floor-attribution recompute step ('recompute impact_plan from each finding's persisted verification.severity_attributes with the hard-override floor disabled') has no specified fallback for findings whose persisted severity_attributes lack the plan-severity axes (e.g., early plan-v2 sidecars from before the axes shipped), causing those findings to silently score 0.0 floorless impact and never appear floor-driven, potentially under-counting the floor-driven share.
findings.location: What bullet 6 — floor-attribution analysis: 'recompute impact_plan from each finding's persisted verification.severity_attributes with the hard-override floor disabled'
scenarios: Early plan-v2 sidecars (tagged impact_model_version=plan-v2 but from before the verify schema added plan-severity axes) have severity_attributes={} or the old five base attrs only. Floorless recompute returns 0.0 < any block_threshold, so these blocks are NEVER classified floor-driven — undercount.
The floor-driven share is reported to story 7d91-55fc-e07c-4101 as design input; an under-count biases the floor-split decision toward keeping the floor.

findings.finding: The plan references 'the dependent story (item 2)' for recording `ac_unverifiable` hard-floor findings (AC4), but neither a ticket ID nor a title for this floor-split story is given in the plan text. The ticket's single `deps` entry (`0730-6ceb-88c3-430c`) is a closed eval task unrelated to any floor-split story, and no floor-split dependency is discoverable.
findings.location: What section, bullet 5 and AC4: 'Record findings relevant to the ac_unverifiable hard-floor split on the dependent story (item 2)' / 'A comment on the dependent floor-split story records...'

findings.finding: AC#4 requires measuring 'how often the 0.85 floor drove the block' and the 'missing-oracle vs under-specified-oracle mix', but neither concept is defined in the plan, the script, or anywhere in the codebase — 'missing-oracle' and 'under-specified-oracle' are terms whose vocabulary is never specified. Additionally, the calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py script never reads severity_attributes (including ac_unverifiable values) from the sidecar findings, so it cannot derive whether the hard-floor (not raw verifier priority) was the operative cause of a block.
findings.location: AC#4 / What bullet 5 / docs/experiments/calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py

findings.finding: The calibration script's `load()` silently discards non-matching sidecars with no counter, so `main()` cannot emit the 'skipped remainder' that AC1 and What-bullet-1 require. The plan says to 'record the segment size vs total' and AC1 requires '(and the skipped remainder)', but the instrument doesn't produce that count without modification or a separate pre-scan step. The plan does not specify how the implementer is to obtain this number.
findings.location: What bullet 1 / AC1 / docs/experiments/calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py load() lines 40-64
findings.suggested_fix: In `load()`, add a skipped counter and return it alongside `by_ticket`; update `main()` to print `[segmented: N kept, M skipped (different/untagged version)]` on the same line as the corpus summary.

findings.finding: AC#1 requires the replay comment to include 'the skipped remainder' (sidecars excluded by the segment filter), but calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py has no counter tracking skipped sidecars — the load() function silently drops non-matching entries with a bare `continue` and no accumulator, and main() prints only the matched-segment stats. The required output field has no generator in the referenced tool.
findings.location: AC#1 / docs/experiments/calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py lines 44-51, 87-91
findings.suggested_fix: In load(), add `skipped = 0` before the loop and `skipped += 1` on the `continue` branch, then return `(by_ticket, skipped)`. In main(), unpack and print `skipped` alongside the corpus line.

findings.finding: The script `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py` cannot produce the 'skipped remainder' count required by AC #1: `load()` silently skips non-matching sidecars with no counter, and `main()` prints only the post-filter segment size. The AC requires both the segment counts AND the skipped remainder to be recorded, but the named tool has no implementation path to emit the latter.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #1; docs/experiments/calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py lines 41-51, 87-91
findings.suggested_fix: In `load()`, accumulate `skipped = 0` and increment it on each `continue`; return `(by_ticket, skipped)` and have `main()` also print `total={len(revs)+skipped}, skipped={skipped}` alongside the segment line.

findings.finding: The plan's edit target for threshold changes is named as `src/rebar/llm/criteria/` (the shared criteria machinery layer), but that directory contains no `block_threshold` values — the actual thresholds live in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json`. The ticket's own `file_impact` field correctly names `criteria_routing.json`, but the plan body diverges, pointing an executor to the wrong directory.
findings.location: What section, bullet 4: 'Apply warranted changes to the criteria registry thresholds (src/rebar/llm/criteria/)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the bullet to: 'Apply warranted changes to the criteria registry thresholds (`src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json`) citing the replay evidence; if no change is warranted, record the no-change decision with rationale.'

findings.finding: The plan names `src/rebar/llm/criteria/` as the edit target for applying warranted threshold changes (What bullet 4, AC#3), but confirmed inspection shows that directory contains only ids.py, model.py, and overlay.py — none of which hold block_threshold values. The actual threshold registry is src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json. This is an instruction-locality error: the referenced edit location resolves to the wrong file.
findings.location: What section bullet 4 / src/rebar/llm/criteria/ / src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json

findings.finding: 'Non-trivial' segment size used as a go/no-go gate in the first What bullet has no quantitative floor, making it unverifiable: an executor cannot determine when the segment is large enough to proceed.
findings.location: What section, first bullet: 'verify...that the segmented corpus is non-trivial'
findings.suggested_fix: 'verify that the segmented corpus contains ≥ 25 sidecars; if below this floor, record the count and defer all threshold changes per the MIN_N deferral rule.'

findings.finding: AC4 contains the hedge 'if derivable' for the missing-oracle vs under-specified-oracle mix, making the criterion conditionally satisfiable without specifying the required deliverable when the mix is NOT derivable — the done state is ambiguous.
findings.location: AC4: 'missing-oracle vs under-specified-oracle mix if derivable'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC4: '...missing-oracle vs under-specified-oracle mix where derivable from sidecar metadata; where not derivable, record that distinction as unknown and state the raw ac_unverifiable hard-floor fire count and rate.'

findings.finding: The adjudication decision rule for the 'keep/raise/lower' outcome (What bullet 3) references signals (validity, rev_rr, p90, fire rate, n vs MIN_N) but never specifies the protocol for the 'raise' direction: under what signal combination should a currently-0.60 criterion be raised to 0.70 (or higher)? The script's proposal logic only emits BLOCK-ELIGIBLE/FP-PRONE/LOW-DATA/ADVISORY-KEEP — it has no 'raise existing threshold' branch. The adjudicator is left without a co-located rule for one of the three named outcomes.
findings.location: What section, bullet 3 / docs/experiments/calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py lines 219-241

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
